prompts.title: Refactor Java class
prompts.description: Refactor a Java class to follow SOLID principles and clean architecture.
prompts.prompt:
You are a senior Java engineer. Refactor the following Java class to improve readability, maintainability, and adherence to SOLID principles.

Requirements:
- Apply the Single Responsibility Principle: extract distinct concerns into separate classes.
- Use dependency injection (constructor injection) instead of direct instantiation.
- Replace any magic numbers or strings with named constants.
- Ensure all public methods have clear, intention-revealing names.
- Prefer composition over inheritance where appropriate.
- Add Javadoc to every public method.
- If the class violates the Open/Closed Principle, introduce interfaces or abstractions.
- Preserve existing behaviour — this is a refactor, not a feature change.

Original class:
```java
[paste your Java class here]
```

Return the refactored code with a short summary of every change you made and why.
prompts.author: digvijay

prompts.title: Spring Boot REST endpoint
prompts.description: Generate a production-ready Spring Boot REST endpoint with validation and error handling.
prompts.prompt:
You are a senior Spring Boot engineer. Generate a complete, production-ready REST endpoint for the following resource.

Resource: [describe the resource, e.g. "User profile with fields: id, name, email, role"]

Requirements:
- Use Spring Boot 3+ with Jakarta EE annotations.
- Create: Controller, Service interface + implementation, Repository (Spring Data JPA), DTO (request + response), and Entity.
- Controller must use @RestController, proper HTTP methods, and return ResponseEntity.
- Add Bean Validation annotations on the request DTO (@NotBlank, @Email, @Size, etc.).
- Service layer must handle business logic; controller stays thin.
- Add a @ControllerAdvice global exception handler that returns RFC 7807 ProblemDetail.
- Use MapStruct or manual mapping between Entity ↔ DTO.
- Include pagination support on the list endpoint (Pageable).
- Add OpenAPI @Operation annotations on each controller method.
- Follow package structure: controller / service / repository / dto / entity / exception.

Return all files with their full package paths.
prompts.author: digvijay

prompts.title: Angular standalone component
prompts.description: Scaffold a modern Angular standalone component with best practices.
prompts.prompt:
You are a senior Angular engineer (Angular 17+). Create a standalone component for the following UI element.

Component: [describe the component, e.g. "A data table with sortable columns, pagination, and row selection"]

Requirements:
- Use standalone: true (no NgModule).
- Use the new control flow syntax (@if, @for, @switch) instead of *ngIf/*ngFor.
- Use signals for component state where appropriate.
- Inputs via input() function, outputs via output() function.
- Use OnPush change detection strategy.
- Write semantic HTML with ARIA attributes for accessibility.
- Component styles must be encapsulated (ViewEncapsulation.Emulated is fine).
- Use trackBy equivalent in @for loops.
- If the component needs async data, accept an Observable or Signal input and handle loading/error states.
- Export the component so it can be imported directly in other standalone components.

Return:
1. component.ts (logic + template using inline template if < 30 lines, else separate)
2. component.scss
3. component.spec.ts (at least 3 meaningful test cases using TestBed)
4. Brief usage example showing how to import and use the component.
prompts.author: digvijay

prompts.title: RxJS Angular service
prompts.description: Create an Angular service with proper RxJS patterns and state management.
prompts.prompt:
You are a senior Angular engineer. Create an Angular injectable service that manages state using RxJS.

Domain: [describe the domain, e.g. "Shopping cart with add, remove, update quantity, and total calculation"]

Requirements:
- Use @Injectable({ providedIn: 'root' }) for tree-shakable singleton.
- Manage internal state with a BehaviorSubject; expose it as a readonly Observable via asObservable().
- All state mutations must go through dedicated public methods (never expose the Subject directly).
- Use appropriate RxJS operators: map, distinctUntilChanged, shareReplay, combineLatest, switchMap, catchError, retry.
- Handle HTTP calls with HttpClient; return Observables (do not subscribe inside the service — let the consumer decide).
- Implement proper error handling: catchError with meaningful error types, not generic strings.
- Add a destroy/cleanup pattern using takeUntilDestroyed() or DestroyRef.
- Write JSDoc for every public method and property.
- Include a companion .spec.ts file with tests using jasmine-marbles or manual subscribe + done() patterns.

Return the complete service file and its spec file.
prompts.author: digvijay

prompts.title: JUnit 5 unit tests
prompts.description: Generate thorough JUnit 5 unit tests with Mockito following the AAA pattern.
prompts.prompt:
You are a senior Java engineer specializing in testing. Write comprehensive JUnit 5 unit tests for the following class.

Class under test:
```java
[paste your Java class here]
```

Requirements:
- Use JUnit 5 (org.junit.jupiter) and Mockito (@ExtendWith(MockitoExtension.class)).
- Follow the Arrange-Act-Assert (AAA) pattern in every test. Add // Arrange, // Act, // Assert comments.
- Use @Mock for dependencies and @InjectMocks for the class under test.
- Cover: happy path, edge cases, null inputs, empty collections, boundary values, and exception scenarios.
- Use @DisplayName with human-readable test names describing the scenario and expected outcome.
- Use @ParameterizedTest with @CsvSource or @MethodSource where multiple inputs test the same logic.
- Use assertThrows for exception testing; verify the exception message.
- Use Mockito.verify() to assert interactions with mocks where relevant.
- Use @Nested classes to group related test scenarios.
- Aim for ≥ 90% branch coverage.
- Do not test private methods directly — test them through public API.

Return the complete test class, ready to compile and run.
prompts.author: digvijay

prompts.title: Cypress E2E flow
prompts.description: Write a complete Cypress end-to-end test for a user workflow.
prompts.prompt:
You are a senior QA automation engineer. Write a complete Cypress E2E test for the following user flow.

Flow: [describe the user flow, e.g. "User registers, verifies email, logs in, updates profile, and logs out"]

Requirements:
- Use Cypress 13+ with TypeScript.
- Use data-cy selectors exclusively (never CSS classes or element tag selectors).
- Structure: describe() block with beforeEach() for setup, individual it() blocks per step.
- Use cy.intercept() to stub or wait on API calls; assert on request payloads and response status.
- Use custom Cypress commands for reusable actions (e.g., cy.login(), cy.fillForm()).
- Add assertions after every significant user action — don't just click through.
- Handle loading states: use cy.get('[data-cy=...]').should('be.visible') before interacting.
- Clean up test data in before() or after() hooks if needed.
- Use cy.fixture() for test data instead of hardcoding strings.
- Include a fixtures/*.json file with sample test data.
- Test both happy path and at least one error/validation scenario.
- No cy.wait(milliseconds) — use proper Cypress waits (intercept aliases, assertions).

Return:
1. The spec file (cypress/e2e/[flow-name].cy.ts)
2. Any custom commands (cypress/support/commands.ts additions)
3. Fixture files (cypress/fixtures/*.json)
prompts.author: digvijay

prompts.title: Design pattern advice
prompts.description: Get expert advice on which design pattern best solves your specific problem.
prompts.prompt:
You are a senior software architect with deep knowledge of GoF design patterns, enterprise patterns, and modern architectural patterns.

I have the following design problem:
[describe your problem, e.g. "I need to support multiple payment providers (Stripe, PayPal, bank transfer) and easily add new ones without modifying existing code"]

Context:
- Language/framework: [e.g. Java 21 / Spring Boot 3]
- Current architecture: [e.g. layered monolith, microservices]
- Constraints: [e.g. must be backward compatible, high throughput, team is junior]

Please:
1. Recommend the most appropriate design pattern(s). Explain why they fit this specific problem.
2. Show a concrete implementation in [language] with realistic class/method names from my domain.
3. Explain the trade-offs: what do I gain, what complexity does it add, when would this pattern be overkill?
4. If there are common anti-patterns or mistakes when implementing this pattern, warn me.
5. Provide a UML-style text diagram (ASCII or Mermaid) showing the class relationships.
6. Suggest how to test the pattern effectively (what to mock, what to integration-test).

Be opinionated — recommend one primary pattern rather than listing five options.
prompts.author: digvijay

prompts.title: Conventional commit message
prompts.description: Generate a well-structured conventional commit message from a diff.
prompts.prompt:
You are a senior engineer who follows the Conventional Commits specification strictly.

Here is my git diff:
```diff
[paste your git diff here]
```

Generate a commit message following these rules:
- Format: <type>(<scope>): <subject>
- Types: feat, fix, refactor, test, docs, style, chore, perf, ci, build
- Scope: the module, component, or area affected (e.g. auth, cart, api)
- Subject: imperative mood, lowercase, no period at the end, max 72 characters
- Body (if needed): explain WHY the change was made, not WHAT (the diff shows what). Wrap at 72 characters.
- Footer (if needed): BREAKING CHANGE: description, or Closes #issue-number
- If the diff contains multiple logical changes, suggest splitting into separate commits and provide each message.

Return ONLY the commit message(s), ready to paste into the terminal. No explanation needed unless suggesting a split.
prompts.author: digvijay

prompts.title: Debug this error
prompts.description: Systematically debug an error with root cause analysis and fix suggestions.
prompts.prompt:
You are a senior full-stack debugger. Systematically analyse the following error and help me fix it.

Error message / stack trace:
```
[paste the error message and full stack trace here]
```

Relevant code (if available):
```
[paste the relevant code section]
```

Context:
- Language/Framework: [e.g. Java 21, Spring Boot 3.2, Angular 17]
- When it happens: [e.g. on startup, when calling endpoint X, after deploying to prod]
- What changed recently: [e.g. upgraded a dependency, added a new feature, nothing]

Please:
1. **Root cause**: Explain what the error means in plain English and identify the root cause (not just the symptom).
2. **Why it happens**: Explain the chain of events that leads to this error.
3. **Fix**: Provide the exact code change to fix it. Show before and after.
4. **Verify**: How to confirm the fix works (command to run, test to write).
5. **Prevent**: How to prevent this class of error in the future (linter rule, test, type safety, etc.).
6. **Related issues**: Mention if this error is commonly caused by other things I should also check.

Be specific. Don't give generic advice — tailor everything to the exact error shown.
prompts.author: digvijay

prompts.title: PR description
prompts.description: Generate a thorough pull request description from a diff or branch summary.
prompts.prompt:
You are a senior engineer writing a pull request description for your team.

Here is the diff or summary of changes:
```
[paste your git diff, commit log, or summary of changes]
```

Jira/Issue ticket: [ticket ID or "none"]

Generate a PR description with these sections:

## What
One paragraph summarising what this PR does. Be specific — mention the feature, endpoint, component, or bug by name.

## Why
Explain the motivation. Link to the ticket/issue. Why is this change needed now?

## How
Brief technical summary of the approach. Mention key design decisions, patterns used, or trade-offs made.

## Testing
- List the manual testing steps a reviewer should follow.
- Mention which automated tests were added or modified.
- Note any edge cases explicitly tested.

## Screenshots / Evidence
[placeholder for screenshots if UI change, or curl commands / API responses if backend]

## Checklist
- [ ] Unit tests pass
- [ ] E2E tests pass (if applicable)
- [ ] No new lint warnings
- [ ] Documentation updated (if applicable)
- [ ] Backward compatible (or migration documented)
- [ ] Reviewed own diff before requesting review

Keep the tone professional but not overly formal. Aim for a description that lets a reviewer understand the full context in under 2 minutes.
prompts.author: digvijay
